speaker: interviewer
text: With Alfred Adler in Tamarac, Florida on November 21, 1995. Now, when you left the hospital, what happened--

speaker: interviewee
text: I looked--

speaker: interviewer
text: Occupationally?

speaker: interviewee
text: I looked for different employment, and found employment in Manhattan as a shipping clerk in a garment-- garment company, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] for which I worked some-- somewhere like eight years. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No, I'm-- pardon me, I'm sorry. I was there two years. And then I found employment in a-- in a place on Fifth Avenue in New York where they sold figurines, glassware, crystal. And I was there for about six years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of that company?

speaker: interviewee
text: Martin Freeman Company, 225 Fifth Avenue

speaker: interviewer
text: And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I-- I saw an ad in a paper, and I applied for Georg Jensen, Fifth Avenue. And I was hired, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] originally as a service manager, and worked my way up as the store manager. And I was there for nine years.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's the famous Georg Jensen?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: Danish?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: Silver.

speaker: interviewee
text: Danish furniture, silver, diamonds, et cetera, et cetera.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel qualified for that job with your background?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was a fast learner, and they noticed it. And I had a-- I had a break.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where-- after Georg Jensen, what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: After Georg Jensen, Jensen moved at that time to a smaller location. And the chairman of the board and most of the board became old. And it changed. And I knew it came to an end, so I went to Rosenthal China [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] till I-- till I made the move to Florida.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year did you move to Florida?

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're here 24 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were living in New York, where did you live most of the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Manhattan.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Manhattan. Did you marry again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you marry?

speaker: interviewer
text: And who did you marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean, the name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: A woman by the name of Friedel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maiden name or--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, maiden name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maiden name Sender, S-E-N-D-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she from Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What part of Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schweich, S-C-H-W-E-I-C-H-- on the Moselle.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you both moved to Florida.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the house I am in, or the-- to the house we are in.

speaker: interviewer
text: This very house in Tamarac.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Now you're in Florida. What did you do when you got here?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I did when I got here. I had friends in New York and he called me one day to get him an application for the police department-- for the sheriff's department-- in Miami. I went there. They didn't give me the application, because he would have to come in person. But instead of me not getting the application for him, I took one for myself and handed it in and was asked if I would like to make the test. They have an opening the same afternoon, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Which I did. I was told then that within five and seven days, I will be notified, and this was the end of it as far as I'm concerned. I wasn't serious about this. I got a notification in five days that my test came out with a 96 markup and that I will get called shortly for an interview, which I-- I-- I was. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] They called me for an interview, And I was sent to Jackson Memorial Hospital, I think two or three days later, for a physical. The physical came out all right with one exception, that I had a right branch bundle block. And they were not serious about it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they sent me to a cardiologist. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I went to the cardiologist and got a clean bill of health. I was told to report for work a week-- week later. At that time, we hadn't even moved yet. I flew back to New York, helped my wife moving-- packing. Came back and started to work the following Monday. I was assigned to the maximum security Dade County jail.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in that job?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked in Mi-- Miami in the Dade County for a year and a half because at that time, they-- the age-- I made the age limit-- excuse me-- in Miami. But I didn't make it in Broward County. A year and a half later, they opened up in Broward County, and I made all the tests up here, including a seven-hour psychological evaluation. And I made high marks in the test and was hired here for the sheriff's department.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your title?

speaker: interviewee
text: Deputy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Deputy Sheriff in Broward County.

speaker: interviewee
text: Correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till 1976, at which time I made my real estate license.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you sold real estate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Commercial or residential?

speaker: interviewee
text: Both. Both.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Tamarac?

speaker: interviewee
text: All over Broward.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from that job, you retired.

speaker: interviewee
text: From that job, I retired, with the exception that I did 12 years' volunteer work at Holy Cross Hospital in Fort Lauderdale, and 10 years with the City of Tamarac [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the Consumer Affairs Department, five years of which I was the chairman of the department. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Plus 2 years as president of the Citizens Band Radio in Broward County.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the temple? Do you belong to a temple here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was one of the founders of our temple.

speaker: interviewer
text: The name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Temple Beth Torah in Tamarac. But I have since resigned a long time ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the name of the rabbi in the temple?

speaker: interviewee
text: At present? It, as far as I know, is Dr. [? Gross. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: You've had a very varied-- very varied-- career.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, Alfred, why did you decide to do this interview with us?

speaker: interviewee
text: The reason being is that it hurts me terribly when I hear people say it did not happen. When I see and hear people, like Farrakhan, and at times, Jesse Jackson, open their big mouths without knowing what the real truth of the-- of the matter is. That is the [? onliest ?] reason. And so that our next generation knows that it should never happen again.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you did not have children, did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I did-- Not with the exception of the one boy in China.

speaker: interviewer
text: The one boy in China. What happened to your wife, [? Friedel? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: She, unfortunately, died in 1991 after a stroke surgery.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here in Holy Cross, Fort Lauderdale.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever find out what happened to your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had applied to the American Red Cross and they notified me from time to time that it's still pending. And in 1993, I got a notification that they were deported to Auschwitz and died in Auschwitz in the spring of 40-- of 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think it took so long, until 1993, to get that information?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, because I-- I assume that the Red Cross had an enormous amount of in-- inquiries. And the Russians only gave them a listing, I believe, beginning of '93 or end of '92. So I'm very grateful-- grateful for them, whatever they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: You think with the fall of communism in Russia, these files were released?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I'm-- I am convinced about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, Alfred, is there anything else you want to add that you feel you want to tell us now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have one cousin who are snow birds in-- in Florida. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And lucky enough that one of them has arrived and is here. And I have another cousin who lives in Long-- on Long Island. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I have another cousin who lives and is alive in New York, but I don't see much of him. He's a very old, but very wealthy man. And I was lucky that I have found a nice lady, whom I'm engaged to. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you plan to marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, these cousins that you're talking about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they survivors of the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: All of them, the three of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The ones who are alive, yes. And all the rest are dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how many people in your family, would you say, perished in the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: If I have-- if I can give you an estimate, it would be at least 20.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you don't know where, under what circumstances.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I know that they vanished in Auschwitz. This I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. And their family name was Adler, most of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not all of them. Some of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, Fred, we want to thank you so much for giving this valuable-- very valuable-- and unusual testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: You are most welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: This testimony was remarkable.

speaker: interviewee
text: You are most welcome, and I hope it helps some other people later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: It surely will.

speaker: interviewee
text: This happens to be my cousin, Trude, Trudy Reinhard, whom I spoke previously that she's a snow bird, part time in Kew Gardens, New York, and they have a condo in Margate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Trude, do you want to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm here about five years in that place, and we come down for about--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you come from in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Europe, I came in Frankfurt-- near Frankfurt am Main in Germany, and I left in '39, Au-- end of August, just before the war. And it was lucky to get out, because I had nobody there. But through the German [? office ?] state aid, they placed us with people as a domestic. That's the only way I could come out. And-- And I stayed there for about nine years during the Blitz. Very lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: In London?

speaker: interviewee
text: Outside London. East London. And-- but I had a sister here and she knew my husband's father. And so when she saw him, she says, you know, your son-- I met my husband in Germany once. He don't remember, but I do. So she said, you know, he promised to help us get my sister from England to here. So she-- he said, well, I talked to him, and he was willing to put-- put partly to a-- what do they call--

speaker: interviewer
text: Affidavit.

speaker: interviewee
text: Affidavit. And I came-

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, in London-- excuse me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience any bombing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, matter of fact, we were one time in an Anderson shelter that's in the back yard. And I must have just fallen asleep, and all of a sudden, I noticed that things came raining down. There was a bomb across the street, and a couple of people got killed. But otherwise, it wasn't too bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they German rockets? [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the buzz bombs.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you very much, Trude.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: We appreciate your coming here today.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here we go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This happens to be my fiance, Eve. Eve Tulsky.

speaker: interviewer
text: T-U-L-S-K-Y.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long do you know each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: We know each other almost a year and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eve is American-born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Chicago-born, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I have to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead. Explain.

speaker: interviewee
text: This happened to be pictures of my parents from years back. I don't know what year. This--

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: This particular one, I might not even have been born yet. Their name is-- my father's first name was Nathan, and my mother's name was Jenny. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Obviously, on this particular picture, I had been born. [LAUGHS] And I believe I was two years old. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] Here, I was skiing on a weekend trip. This happened to be the fam-- the famous Olympic town, Garmisch-Partenkirchen in German-- in the German Alps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Alfred, who was the girl in these pictures-- the woman in these pictures?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those are different ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, leave it to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, this-- this girl is on many.

speaker: interviewer
text: Alfred, these are letters that you sent?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, which I sent from the concentration camp to my mother or father at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what year? I'm sure there's a date on there. Is that your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that's the birth date. But I saw before-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: There's a date there. 15Th of January.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Is that your handwriting? So you were saying they told you what to write.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you want to explain about the letters?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, except that these postcards [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] or letters, which we-- which we were allowed to write once a month, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] with the understanding that everything is cens-- being censored, and you could only write what they told you to write, that everything is in fine shape.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is roughly '38, 1938, 1939.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is between '38 and '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This particular stamp is from the 17th of January, 1939, out of Dachau. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This was a stamp from a le-- a letter, not a card, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] addressed to my [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] family at that time, whoever was there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this, what you see on your left in print, are the rules and regulations from the concentration camp. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I just noticed you were allowed two letters or two cards, what I can read from there. [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: [SPEAKING GERMAN]

speaker: interviewee
text: You are not allowed-- you were not allowed to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] get any packages, as you can buy everything you wanted to at the commissary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you make out that stamp at the bottom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This was the stamp that it was censored. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It's official. That's the way-- [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] This is from [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the 15th of January, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1938. I-- I-- I think this is a mistake.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here's another date.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- I can tell you what this is. This is the 17th of November, '38.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was just turning Jan-- '39.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was the first card I was able to send to my mother, telling them-- telling her that I'm in there and that I found my father in the camp, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was a bigger letter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was a letter. The other-- the other ones were postal cards.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] concentration camp here, which picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken a few months after I came out, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as I can see my hair grew back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead and explain this.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in 1939 when I was a member of the Shanghai International Police Department. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I was there for the following three years. And left-- when I left, I had the rank of Lance Corporal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead. Say that again.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am the one on the right-hand side of this picture. Or, in this case, the skinny one. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] This was my Chinese wife, Rose, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who later on, unfortunately, got kidnapped by the Chinese communists and I was not able, to this very day, to get any further information of their whereabouts or deaths or whatever happened to them. Also, my boy of seven years was kidnapped at the same time together with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were they kidnapped?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were nationalists, supporters of Chiang Kai-shek. She was a famous movie actress, and it must have been a political move. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a family picture. My mother, her sister, and two brothers, and my niece. All of them [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] died in the concentration camp. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] That's a picture of mine somewhere in a outdoor cafe in China. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] This is a picture of my parents, which must be quite old when it was taken. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture of my late wife and myself, taken years ago in Atlantic City on a vacation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my wife, Friedel, with our 1957 Cadillac, I believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken, I believe, in 1989, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] at which time we both were still very happy. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] And this is my fiancee, Eve, and it was taken at her son's home in Northbrook, Illinois, in 1995. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That's it.
